preamble: Palatalization and affrication of the sequence [tj] (from historical [t] + [u/ju]) into the voiceless post-alveolar affricate [tʃ] (represented here as 'ch') is a key feature of Early Modern English phonology.

examples.note: Historical palatalization of the dental stop triggered by the following 'u' (specifically its palatal glide onset).
Merge of the alveolar stop and the palatal glide into an affricate.
Common affricated pronunciation in several British and Australian varieties (compared to American 'toon').
